html:
RECOUP Intensive Spinal Deformity Rehabilitation Program

Location: RECOUP Hospital, Anjanapura Layout; RECOUP Clinics in Jayanagar and Bellandur, Bengaluru, India

What are Spinal Deformities?

Spinal deformity refers to an abnormality in the spine's curves. It encompasses deformations in the coronal plane, known as scoliosis, and those in the sagittal plane, called kyphosis. Scoliosis is characterized by lateral curvature of the spine, torsion of the spine and chest, and a disturbance in the sagittal profile. The Cobb angle is used to quantify the magnitude of spinal curvature on a spinal X-ray.

Why should spinal deformities be treated?

If the Cobb angle at completion of growth exceeds a "critical threshold" (most experts assume it to be between 30° and 50°), there is a higher risk of health problems in adult life, decreased quality of life, cosmetic deformity, significant disability, pain and progressive functional limitations.

There are 3 scientifically accepted methods of treatment of spinal deformities:

  1. Rehabilitation
  2. Corrective Bracing
  3. Spinal Fusion Surgery

The International Society on Scoliosis Orthopaedic and Rehabilitation Treatment (SOSORT) guidelines:

  1. An interdisciplinary team comprising rehabilitation physicians, physiotherapists, orthotists, clinical psychologists, and other specialists must collaboratively deliver the rehabilitation and follow-up.
  2. General physiotherapy or exercises are not recommended.
  3. Rehabilitation requires physicians and physiotherapists to be specifically trained and certified in PSSE.

Physiotherapy Scoliosis Specific Exercises (PSSE)

PSSE is a component of a scoliosis care model that encompasses scoliosis-specific education, scoliosis-specific physical therapy exercises, observation or surveillance, and psychological support and intervention.

  1. Schroth Best Practice (Germany)
  2. Schroth Barcelona Scoliosis Physical Therapy School (Spain)
  3. SEAS - Scientific Exercise Approach to Scoliosis (Italy)
  4. Dobomed (Poland)
  5. FITS - Functional Individual Therapy of Scoliosis (Poland)
  6. Lyon (France)
  7. Side Shift (UK)

Dr Deepak Sharan is the world's first and only physician to be trained/certified in all seven PSSE.

SHARAN'S Approach to Scoliosis Rehabilitation

Skilled

Hands-on

Active

Realignment with

Awareness of Body and Breathing and

Neuroplasticity for

Scoliosis

This advanced rehabilitation approach incorporates elements of all PSSE methods and several modern, evidence-based techniques to optimize muscular, respiratory, and sensorimotor function. Orthotics are sometimes utilized.

Aims of RECOUP Intensive Spinal Deformity Rehabilitation Program (RISDRP):

  1. To improve aesthetics and facilitate correction of the 3-D trunk shape and global posture
  2. To teach the patient to maintain the corrected posture in daily activities
  3. To slow or stop curvature progression (Cobb angle)
  4. To help patients cope with the deformity and with treatment, and improve psychological wellbeing, improve self-image and quality of life
  5. To diminish functional limitations
  6. To address joint impairments (hyper flexibility and hypo flexibility)
  7. To address muscle impairments (flexibility, weakness, endurance deficits)
  8. To improve neuromotor control (balance, autocorrection control, and muscle activation imbalances)
  9. To treat pain
  10. To improve pulmonary function (vital capacity)

Principles of RISDRP

  1. Education
  2. Active,3-D self-correction
  3. Training in activities of daily living (ADL)
  4. Stabilizing the corrected posture
  5. Neuroplasticity enables the brain to adapt and compensate for the changes in spinal alignment and movement patterns. This adaptability allows the brain to reorganize neural pathways, relearn motor skills, modulate pain perception and maintain functional stability despite the structural changes of scoliosis.

Which types of spinal deformities are suitable for RISDRP?

  • Adolescents and Adults with Idiopathic Scoliosis
  • Juvenile Kyphosis
  • Postural abnormalities (e.g., upper and lower crossed patterns)
  • Syndromic and Neuromuscular Scoliosis with "idiopathic-like pattern"

Limitations (RISDRP can sometimes be used)

  • Syndromic and Neuromuscular Scoliosis
  • Painful Adult Degenerative Scoliosis
  • Osteoporosis
  • Infantile and Juvenile Idiopathic Scoliosis
  • Post-surgery
  • Spondylolisthesis
  • Inflammatory disease during the non-active phase

Contraindications (RISDRP is not advised)

  • Reactive scoliosis (Tumor, Disc Herniation, Infection, etc.)
  • Inflammatory disease during the active phase
  • Psychiatric problems (hysteria, psychosis, etc.)

How frequently is therapy and follow-up required?

  • Patients living in Bengaluru who can travel to RECOUP centres may attend 90-minute outpatient sessions on Saturdays and Sundays and daily during vacations.
  • Other patients are advised to initially undergo intensive inpatient rehabilitation for at least a month and attend follow-up rehabilitation for 3-5 days every 3-6 months until further notice.

Facilities at RECOUP for Spinal Deformities Management

RECOUP offers one of the world's most comprehensive, holistic and cutting-edge spine deformity rehabilitation programs. It features a physician-led interdisciplinary rehabilitation team and state-of-the-art facilities.

  • Consultation, supervision and follow-up with the Orthopedic Surgeon and the Rehabilitation Physician, who is specialized and certified in rehabilitating scoliosis.
  • Objective clinical measurements using Bunnell's Scoliometer, Inclinometers, Instrumented Posture and Gait Analysis, Foot Pressure Analysis, Plumb Lines, LASER, and relevant radiological measurements.
  • Comprehensive evaluation focusing on body morphology and symptomatology, examination of muscle retractions associated with posture alterations, and re-equilibration tests to assess scoliosis reducibility.
  • Computerized documentation using Scoliosis Manager® for systematic monitoring of outcomes, assessment of the patient's co-operation, and verification and modification of methods during the therapy.
  • Spine deformity-specific back school (education).

RECOUP's Interdisciplinary Spinal Deformities Rehabilitation Team

  • Orthopedic Surgeon
  • Rehabilitation Physicians
  • Physiotherapists
  • Occupational Therapists
  • Aquatic Therapists
  • Clinical Psychologists
  • Nutritionists
  • Orthotists
  • Yoga and Naturopathy Physicians
  • Functional Medicine Physicians
  • Lifestyle Medicine Physicians
  • Health Coaches
  • Rehabilitation Nurses
  • Rehabilitation Coordinator
  • Other specialists as required

RECOUP's Spinal Deformities Rehabilitation Approaches

  1. Schroth Best Practice (SBP) is the predominant method used at RECOUP. The SBP utilizes a 3-D approach that focuses on correcting the posture, improving breathing mechanics, and strengthening muscles to stabilize the spine. The core principles involve addressing the pelvis, performing rotational angular breathing (RAB), and strengthening trunk muscles through isometric contractions.
  2. The Schroth Barcelona Scoliosis Physical Therapy School is based on 3-D postural correction and the expansion/contraction technique to achieve the best possible 3-D trunk shape and spinal alignment (geometry). It is supported by breathing mechanics, and stabilization through muscle tension involves isometric, anisometric, eccentric, and concentric tension and integration.
  3. Scientific Exercise Approach to Scoliosis (SEAS) is based on a scoliosis-specific active self-correction technique performed without any external aids and incorporated into functional exercises.
  4. The Dobomed approach combines both Klapp's position for kyphotization of the thoracic spine and Lehnert-Schroth's method for active asymmetrical breathing.
  5. Functional Individual Therapy of Scoliosis (FITS) emphasizes patient awareness, myofascial release, and functional movement patterns.
  6. The Lyon method combines PSSE with the Lyon ART brace (Asymmetrical Rigid Torsion brace) and casting. The physiotherapeutic treatment includes 3-D mobilization of the spine and mobilization of the iliolumbar angle (lumbar scoliosis), patient education, and ADL, which includes correcting the sitting position.
  7. Side Shift: A flexible curve can be stabilized with lateral movements. Excessive side movement of the trunk corrects the lateral deviation of the trunk along the coronal plane.
  8. Coordination Dynamics Therapy (CDT) focuses on using movements to help the body reorganize its posture and enhance coordination. It involves rhythmic and dynamic movements, often in an anti-gravity position, to stimulate the central nervous system and encourage new movement patterns. By improving the phase and frequency coordination of neuron firing and promoting neural plasticity, CDT aims to correct habitual movement patterns and enhance posture.
  9. Mary Massery's "soda pop can model" links breathing mechanics (including the vocal folds) to postural control.
  10. The Marcotte Method focuses on restoring the spine's natural curvatures in the sagittal plane. It integrates elements from yoga, Chiropractic BioPhysics, and the SpineCor method.
  11. The Rovatti Method employs elastic bands to gently reshape the spine, fostering active patient involvement in the correction process and promoting self-correction through targeted exercises.
  12. Manual therapy (myofascial manipulation, fascial manipulation, fascial distortion model) aims to enhance joint mobility, decrease muscle tension, and promote improved spinal alignment through hands-on techniques such as joint mobilization, soft tissue mobilization, and manual stretching.
  13. The Dart Procedures (Evolution of Movement) are a set of movement explorations designed to enhance body awareness and promote freedom of movement. They work by "undoing" unnecessary muscular tension and reducing stiffness to release shortened musculature and improve breathing. The procedures are often described as retracing the evolution of human movement, starting from a fetal position and progressing through various stages of development.
  14. The Alexander Technique improves posture and movement by addressing ingrained habits and muscle tension. It aims to reduce pain, increase flexibility, and enhance overall function by promoting mindful awareness of how the body moves and feels. Core principles include recognizing and correcting postural habits, releasing head, neck, and spine tension, and improving coordination.
  15. The Feldenkrais Method emphasizes improved body awareness and movement efficiency to reduce pain and improve overall function. It focuses on re-educating the nervous system through sensory-motor experiences, rather than forcing specific physical changes.
  16. The Franklin Method, emphasizing embodiment and imagery, can aid individuals with scoliosis in enhancing spinal health, alignment, and overall well-being. It achieves this through dynamic neurocognitive imagery, which merges visualization techniques with physical exercises to improve movement, flexibility, and strength. This approach enables individuals to comprehend how their body is meant to move, resulting in better spinal function and alignment.
  17. Iyengar Yoga focuses on aligning, strengthening, and lengthening the spine to reduce the curve. It emphasizes precise body positioning in each pose, using props to support and adjust, and remaining in the pose long enough for necessary adjustments. This therapeutic approach encourages mind-body awareness and self-empowerment.
  18. Tai Chi promotes improved posture, balance, and flexibility through slow, controlled movements and deep breathing. Key principles include centring, grounding, and maintaining a relaxed, upright posture, which can help to realign the spine and improve overall body awareness.
  19. Pilates addresses spinal alignment, core strength, and mindful movement.
  20. Functional strengthening focuses on exercises that enhance spinal stability, improve postural awareness, and address muscle imbalances caused by the curvature.
  21. Virtual and augmented reality-based therapy utilizes principles of motor learning, immersive engagement, and visual feedback to enhance posture, movement, and overall spinal health. By creating virtual environments where patients can interact with simulated spinal structures and perform exercises, VR and AR therapy assist in retraining the body's proprioception, improving muscle control, and boosting patient motivation and adherence to treatment.
  22. Graded motor imagery employs neuroplasticity to address cortical changes and alleviate pain and disability. It encompasses a three-stage process involving laterality recognition, motor imagery, and mirror therapy to retrain the brain and enhance movement representation.
  23. Mirror biofeedback utilizes the concept of mirror neurons and visual feedback to enhance body awareness and improve postural control. By observing their own movements in a mirror, individuals with scoliosis can learn to consciously correct their posture and engage the muscles on the concave side of their curve, promoting a more symmetrical and balanced spine. This technique aims to enhance muscle re-education and potentially reduce the progression of spinal curvature.
  24. EMG biofeedback provides real-time feedback on muscle electrical activity to help patients consciously learn to control muscle activity, particularly in the paraspinal muscles. This feedback, typically visual or auditory, enables patients to understand their muscle activity patterns and train themselves to modify them, ultimately aiming to reduce asymmetry and manage curve progression.
  25. Whole Body Vibration (WBV) therapy harnesses the body's natural responses to vibration to enhance bone health, boost muscle strength, and potentially slow curve progression. It functions by stimulating muscle contractions and activating the neuromuscular system, which can result in increased bone density and enhanced spinal flexibility.
  26. Exergaming employs games to motivate patients to perform corrective exercises and improve posture. The principles revolve around engaging the player with a game while simultaneously promoting specific exercises that address spinal curvature. Exergames aim to enhance compliance and accuracy in performing scoliosis-specific exercises by making exercise more enjoyable and interactive.
  27. Aquatic Therapy in a temperature-controlled swimming pool leverages the unique properties of water, primarily buoyancy, warmth and resistance, to provide a low-impact, therapeutic exercise environment. Buoyancy reduces stress on the spine and joints, while water resistance helps strengthen muscles without excessive loading.
  28. The Dorn method gently corrects misalignments in the spine and joints, restoring balance and reducing nerve compression. It involves palpation, gentle pressure, and guided patient movements to relax muscles and facilitate the spine's natural repositioning.
  29. The Primal Reflex Release Technique (PRRT) can benefit individuals with scoliosis by addressing pain and muscle spasms and improving function through a neurological approach. PRRT aims to downregulate the autonomic nervous system by identifying and resetting certain reflexes.
  30. Therapeutic taping facilitates muscle activation or inhibition, improves circulation and lymphatic drainage, and provides proprioceptive feedback.
  31. The Kaltenborn-Evjenth concept (OMT) can enhance joint function and alleviate pain. It encompasses various techniques, such as soft tissue mobilization, joint mobilization (traction, compression, gliding), and nerve tissue mobilization.
  32. Hellerwork, a form of structural integration, utilizes deep connective tissue work, movement education, and verbal dialogue to address scoliosis. It aims to improve body alignment, posture, and mobility, potentially reducing pain and stress associated with the condition. Its emphasis on the mind-body connection and resolving emotional blockages can also be beneficial in managing scoliosis-related challenges.
  33. Erik Dalton's Myoskeletal Alignment Techniques integrates deep tissue work, assisted stretching, visceral techniques, and joint mobilization.
  34. Steven Goldstein's Fascia therapy focuses on releasing fascial restrictions and addressing joint dysfunction to enhance spinal alignment, alleviate pain, and potentially stabilize or reduce scoliotic curves.
  35. Global Postural Re-education (GPR) aims to improve body biomechanics, correct postural imbalances, and reduce stiffness by addressing the root causes of scoliosis through a holistic approach. It involves active postures and exercises that realign joints, stretch shortened muscles, and enhance the function of antagonist muscles to promote symmetry and muscle balance.
  36. Hypermobility rehabilitation, e.g., the Muldowney Protocol.
  37. Occupational therapy is crucial in helping individuals with scoliosis manage their condition and maintain their ability to participate in daily activities. They assess and modify daily activities to reduce strain on the spine, teach compensatory strategies, and support improving posture and function.
  38. Psychological approaches (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), Motivational Interviewing, pain reprocessing therapy, emotional awareness and expression therapy) aim to empower individuals to cope with the condition's physical and emotional challenges, improve their quality of life, and promote adherence to treatment plans.
  39. Behavior modification principles focus on promoting desirable behaviors and discouraging undesirable ones to enhance posture, manage pain, and improve overall function. These principles are applied through various techniques, including reinforcement, cueing, and modelling, to help individuals adopt and maintain healthier habits related to their scoliosis.
  40. Lifestyle medicine focuses on using daily healthy habits to manage symptoms and potentially slow their progression. These include a balanced diet, regular physical activity, stress management, and restorative sleep.
  41. The primary principle of orthotics is the three-point pressure system, where a brace exerts pressure on the spine, applying counterforces both above and below the curve to promote correction and stabilization. This system aims to prevent curve progression and potentially avoid or delay the need for surgery.

RISDRP Inpatient Rehabilitation Program

Duration: initially for at least one month, and attend follow-up rehabilitation for three to five days every three to six months until further notice.

The daily treatment is about 4.5 hours, divided into three 1.5-hour sessions with a one-hour break between them.

Goals

  1. To educate patients about their own specific curve type and how it affects their posture,
  2. To learn the principles of the Schroth Best Practice and other selected approaches in activities of daily living (ADL) to assure constant active correction throughout the day,
  3. To increase motivation and commitment,
  4. To teach patients the home exercise program (HEP) aimed at recalibration of the posture affected by scoliosis or kyphosis using their own ability to auto correct in all three planes of the body,
  5. To stabilize the corrected posture by highlighting the importance of adhering to the HEP and ADL.

Patient requirements:

  • Training attire: Female participants must wear a bikini or tank top and comfortable bottoms, while males can be shirtless. Hair should be held so that the shoulders and back are exposed.
  • Braced patients should bring their brace to each training session.
  • Patients must stay for the whole duration of the camp.

Components of the RISDRP Inpatient Rehabilitation Program

  • On the first day of the inpatient program, Dr. Sharan conducts comprehensive individual assessments of patients, and the HEP is prescribed based on each patient's specific curve pattern and abilities. Physiotherapists teach and supervise these exercises.
  • The program will include a thorough examination of all body systems, the spine and posture, including Scoliometer/Inclinometer Cobb angle measurements, rotation of the spine and trunk, formetric scan, a Posture Scan (back symmetry), Spirometer (lung capacity), Photographs (cosmetic improvement), a Visual Analogue Scale (pain levels), Trunk Aesthetic Clinical Evaluation (TRACE), and the Scoliosis Research Society Questionnaire (quality of life). The measurements will be repeated weekly.
  • A series of educational sessions focused on fostering a comprehensive understanding of spinal deformity, spinal anatomy, increasing awareness of correct posture in daily activities, explaining the measurements taken during treatment, outlining the principles of the RISDRP method, and detailing our support system for when they return home.
  • Behavioral coaching and psychological intervention. The patient will be instructed in possible changes to their daily habits according to the curve type and their pain/ function issues.
  • Physical, occupational and aquatic therapy.
  • Lifestyle and nutritional assessment and recommendations.
  • A final consultation with Dr Sharan will be held to discuss the corrections and improvements one has made during the treatment course, as well as further recommendations and follow-up arrangements.

Cost

Outpatient rehabilitation

INR 1000 per session of 45 minutes

Inpatient rehabilitation

INR 100000 per month

INR 60000 per 15 days

Including consultations, clinical measurements, and rehabilitation.

The cost does not include lab or radiological investigations, medicines, orthotics, accommodation, food or transfers.

Patient Testimonials

When my 13-year-old daughter was diagnosed with scoliosis two months ago, with a 55-degree curve, the only option suggested by doctors was correction through surgery.

However, I discovered RECOUP in Bengaluru, which offers intensive scoliosis treatment using the Schroth method and various other integrated techniques.

I was extremely happy with the results observed within two weeks of treatment, as shown below.

Scoliometer reading at the beginning
Thoracic 17deg
Lumbar 11deg

Scoliometer reading at discharge
Thoracic ~ 15deg
Lumbar ~ 10deg

I'm sure that with more practice, I will be able to see significant improvement.

I will surely recommend RECOUP as an alternate option before going for surgery.

Additionally, the team working here is welcoming and knowledgeable. They addressed all my daughter's questions about the treatment and exercises, which heightened her awareness of her own body and her willingness to engage in these corrective exercises.

All the exercises chosen were based on each individual's curve and severity.

I highly recommend consulting RECOUP before going for any surgery.

Kanchan, Mumbai

I'm Tanisha, and I want to share my views on scoliosis and how it has impacted my life.

Scoliosis is a medical condition in which the backbone curves and rotates, typically forming an S-shape or C-shape. It can occur at any age but is most common between the ages of 9 and 16. Scoliosis may be hereditary or result from poor posture. Since I have scoliosis, I have gained extensive information about it from the Internet, Dr. Deepak Sharan, and my physiotherapists at RECOUP.

Reading the internet, I thought that surgery was the only option for my progressive curve, but here, I feel that I can correct my spine without surgery, and I will.

There are a few methods to treat scoliosis without surgery: the Schroth method and the use of a brace. The main idea behind treating scoliosis with the Schroth method is to make the spine flexible. In contrast, surgery typically results in the spine being fused, which can lead to increased stiffness and potentially worsen the condition. Therefore, I believe that exercises and bracing, when used in conjunction, should help to slow the progression of spinal curvature.

In RECOUP, I have learnt a few treatment methods for exercising: Schroth Treatment and Iyengar. Both methods help the body become more flexible. The Schroth Treatment has assisted me with self-correction and shown me how I can improve my posture. Iyengar teaches me how to physically perform several exercises and correct myself.

One very important lesson I learned here was not just to perform the exercises, but to understand and feel my body. Now, I can adjust my spine by simply visualising my backbone in my mind.

I want to thank the physiotherapists who motivated me from the very first day and made me realise that I should never give up and lose hope because all I need is patience and a little bit of hard work to achieve anything.

I want to thank all my physiotherapists here at RECOUP. Now, "Scoliosis is no longer a deformity to me, it's a challenge to myself. "

Tanisha

My daughter has scoliosis. For the last three years, we have visited many doctors and physiotherapists in Delhi NCR, including AIIMS, but haven't seen any improvement.

As a parent, I found it very difficult. We felt helpless and hopeless before visiting RECOUP. Within a few days, she gained 1 cm in height. I have never seen her stand so straight in months. Their exercises are truly effective, also improving rib cage rotation. Many thanks to RECOUP for their efforts in saving my daughter's life.

Sukriti Arora
